keypoints: ⭐ Transitional epithelium (urothelium): lines renal calyces → pelvis → ureters → bladder → proximal urethra; changes shape with distension
Empty bladder: 6-7 cell layers, dome-shaped umbrella cells (surface) containing plaques of asymmetric membrane unit proteins (uroplakins)
Full bladder: epithelium stretches to 2-3 layers, cells flatten; tight junctions prevent urine from entering lamina propria
Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma: most common bladder cancer; arises from transitional epithelium; associated with smoking, aniline dyes, cyclophosphamide
questions.imageAlt: H&E section of urinary bladder wall showing transitional epithelium (urothelium) with dome-shaped surface umbrella cells
questions.imageCaption: H&E section of urinary bladder: transitional epithelium (urothelium) with characteristic dome-shaped umbrella cells at the luminal surface; lamina propria of dense collagenous connective tissue beneath; detrusor smooth muscle visible deeper in the wall

questions.stem: First-year medical students examine the wall of the urinary bladder in the histology laboratory. Identify the tissue indicated by the asterisk.
questions.explanation: The bladder wall is composed of three layers: (1) mucosa (transitional epithelium + lamina propria), (2) muscularis (smooth muscle = detrusor muscle), and (3) adventitia/serosa. The muscularis of the bladder — the detrusor muscle — contains abundant smooth muscle fibers running in multiple directions (inner longitudinal, middle circular, outer longitudinal). The asterisk indicates these interlacing smooth muscle bundles. The detrusor is innervated by parasympathetic fibers from S2–S4; contraction during micturition compresses the bladder and expels urine. Skeletal muscle (choice D) surrounds the external urethral sphincter (membranous urethra) but is not in the bladder wall itself.
keypoints: ⭐ Detrusor muscle = smooth muscle of bladder wall; 3 layers (inner longitudinal/middle circular/outer longitudinal); parasympathetic (S2-S4) = contraction = micturition
Internal urethral sphincter = smooth muscle (involuntary); innervated by sympathetic fibers → contracts to prevent urine leakage and semen reflux
External urethral sphincter = skeletal muscle (voluntary); surrounds membranous urethra; innervated by pudendal nerve (somatic)
Neurogenic bladder: upper motor neuron lesion → spastic bladder; lower motor neuron lesion → flaccid bladder with overflow incontinence
questions.imageAlt: H&E section of urinary bladder wall showing smooth muscle bundles of the detrusor muscle (asterisks)
questions.imageCaption: H&E section of urinary bladder wall: smooth muscle bundles of the detrusor muscle (asterisks) running in multiple directions; smooth muscle cells show elongated nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm; connective tissue (lighter) separates the bundles

questions.stem: Several retroperitoneal organs are examined at autopsy. One of these organs is shown in the image. The histologic features of this specimen suggest that it was obtained from which of the following anatomic locations?
questions.explanation: The characteristic histologic features shown — a stellate (star-shaped) lumen from mucosal folding after smooth muscle contraction at death, transitional epithelium lining the mucosa, inner longitudinal and outer circular smooth muscle layers, and adventitia fusing with retroperitoneal adipose tissue — identify this specimen as the ureter. The stellate lumen is created by the post-mortem contraction of the muscularis, which folds the mucosa. The gallbladder (choice A) is lined by simple columnar epithelium with Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses. The renal artery (choice B) has a muscular arterial wall. The urethra (choice D) and urinary bladder (choice E) have different muscle layer arrangements and lumen shapes.
keypoints: ⭐ Ureter histology: transitional epithelium + lamina propria + inner longitudinal + outer circular smooth muscle + adventitia; stellate lumen post-mortem from muscle contraction
Ureter has 3 layers of smooth muscle in lower 1/3 (inner longitudinal + middle circular + outer longitudinal) to facilitate peristalsis into bladder
Three ureteric constrictions (common sites for stone obstruction): UPJ, crossing of iliac vessels, UVJ (narrowest)
Peristaltic contractions of ureter (3-5/min) propel urine from renal pelvis to bladder against gravity
questions.imageAlt: Low-power H&E cross-section of ureter showing stellate lumen, transitional epithelium, and smooth muscle layers in retroperitoneal fat
questions.imageCaption: H&E cross-section of ureter: characteristic stellate lumen (from post-mortem smooth muscle contraction folding the mucosa), transitional epithelium, inner longitudinal + outer circular smooth muscle layers, and surrounding retroperitoneal adipose tissue (adventitia)

questions.stem: Which portion of the male urethra is surrounded by the external urethral sphincter?
choices: Distal segment of the spongy urethra
Initial segment of the spongy urethra
questions.explanation: The male urethra is approximately 20 cm long and is divided into four anatomic portions: (1) preprostatic urethra — short segment as the bladder opens into the urethra; (2) prostatic urethra — travels through the prostate gland, receiving the ejaculatory ducts and prostatic ducts; (3) membranous urethra — approximately 1 cm, penetrating the deep perineal pouch (urogenital diaphragm); and (4) spongy (penile/cavernous) urethra — approximately 15 cm through the corpus spongiosum of the penis. Voluntary skeletal muscle fibers in the deep perineal pouch wrap around the membranous urethra to form the external urethral sphincter, which provides voluntary control of micturition.
keypoints: ⭐ Male urethra segments: preprostatic (short) → prostatic (through prostate) → MEMBRANOUS (external urethral sphincter, skeletal muscle) → spongy/penile (~15 cm)
External urethral sphincter = SKELETAL muscle, VOLUNTARY control, surrounds MEMBRANOUS urethra, innervated by pudendal nerve (S2-S4)
Internal urethral sphincter = SMOOTH muscle, INVOLUNTARY, at bladder neck, innervated by sympathetic fibers (prevents retrograde ejaculation)
Female urethra = ~4 cm; entirely membranous in equivalent; external sphincter surrounds middle third; shorter → higher UTI risk
